headlines.title: Copper wire theft incidents leave Morgan Hill streets in the dark
headlines.description: Morgan Hill police are investigating two copper wire theft incidents that left some streets in a neighborhood in the dark. Morgan hill police said the first theft happened Saturday and the second early Wednesday morning in the Community Park neighborhood. Police say the thefts caused tens of thousands of dollars in damage and repair costs. “We’ve been here 28 years. I can’t see how somebody could steal all that copper wiring up and down the block without the police or some witness seeing it,” Ernesto Lopez said. Copper wire theft has been an ongoing problem across the Bay Area. Last year, thieves stole copper from lighting along a stretch of interstate 680 in San Jose, leaving parts of the freeway dark. Earlier this year in Richmond, hundreds of streetlights also went dark after copper was stolen. “Copper theft has been an issue for quite a number of years because it’s easy access, and it they can take it to a number of reciclers and get cash, and as we all know metals have done nothing but increase in value,” Law Enforcement Analyst and former SJPD Officer Michael Leininger said. Leininger says preventing these crimes is challenging. “You’re generally relying on recyclers to follow the law and report suspicious persons or incidents,” he said. “Law enforcement, PG&E, the light companies, all need to take a step back and look at how these wires are contained and perhaps come up with a different containment system.” Leininger says surveillance cameras can be a key investigative tool as detectives look for any video that may have captured the thieves. Anyone with information is asked to contact Morgan Hill police.

headlines.title: Oakland Unified School District last to announce state law to limit phone usage
headlines.description: A new California law will require all public schools to have students limit phone usage during the day. Oakland Unified School District is the last to announce this change. For the upcoming academic year, texting, calling and scrolling will be prohibited. Students will be required to store their phones in locked spaces from the beginning to end of the school day. Elementary students will store them in classroom lock boxes. Middle and high school students will lock theirs inside pouches they’ll carry—but won’t be able to open. “It’s going to take some getting used to, there is going to be some growing pains but we are prepared for it,” OUSD spokesperson John Sasaki The state’s goal is to increase face to face interaction, prevent cyberbullying and have less distractions during class. The policy is required for all California public schools, however each district will implement it differently. Some districts, such as Oakland, will ban phone use during passing periods and lunch rather than just class time. Researchers tracked more than 43,000 schools nationwide and found locking phones away cut student phone use, but didn’t lead to immediate improvements in test scores, attendance, or cyberbullying. Co-author of the report Thomas Dee says more research is needed to evaluate long-term benefits. “It may be that phones were never fundamentally the problem or alternatively, it may be that phone bans do not go far enough. We hear from some schools that things like online bullying have simply shifted from phones to laptops and other devices,” he said. Several Bay Area districts that have already gone phone-free say they’ve seen students more engaged in class and a decline in harassment. OUSD’s policy will go into effect on Aug. 10.

headlines.title: South San Jose post office remains closed a year after arson
headlines.description: The Almaden Valley post office in South San Jose remains closed a year after an arson left part of the building destroyed. The arson occurred in July 2025 when Richard Tillman, the brother of former NFL player Pat Tillman, crashed a car with flammable logs into the post office. Based on the building’s appearance today, the post office appears to be a long way out from reopening. Residents and officials have voiced frustration with how long the repair process is taking. “Seems unreasonable that they couldn’t fix the damage in this amount of time,” resident Bob Burriss said. Congressman Jimmy Panetta said he pressured the United States Postal Service to speed up repairs and finally received a completion target date. First it was the spring of 2027, but that has since been pushed to August 2027. In a statement, the USPS said the facility will reopen when the necessary work is completed and it is deemed safe for staff and customers. Residents in the area currently have to travel about six miles to the closest USPS location in Willow Glen.

headlines.title: Ruby Hill families return home after Little Fire forces them to evacuate
headlines.description: The fire line came withing yards of homes in Ruby Hill on Tuesday, prompting evacuation orders. Families in the area had to make some tough decisions as they didn’t know if they were leaving home for a night or potentially for the last time. “All these things you said probably would never happen? Well guess what? In a second, they could happen,” Ruby Hill resident Wendy Mcgee said. That second came Tuesday afternoon, as the Little Fire raced across the hillsides east of Pleasanton, forcing thousands of people from their homes. Just minutes earlier, McGee’s Ring camera captured a police officer at her front door… warning her the flames were closing in. “He said, ‘Yeah, you’re one of the first houses and you’re probably closest to the fire’,” McGee said. “When I turned my back and looked out the back window, I could see the red flames on the hill and I panicked.” As the fire pushed toward the neighborhood, families had to decide what was worth taking and what they had to leave behind. “I came home and I grabbed all my videos, my scrapbooks, my computer, pretty much everything I knew was irreplaceable,” Ruby Hill resident Mokie Marchi said. All this, as they were being told repeatedly to leave. “Our phones went off blaring… evacuate, evacuate, evacuate,” Marchi said. Neighbors checked on one another, some turned on sprinklers and others simply hoped they’d be coming home. By Wednesday, most families were able to return and find their homes still standing. “It was nothing short of a miracle and I looked at it and thought ‘how much do we worry about the little things?’” McGee said. For many of these families, that perspective may be the one thing they’ll carry with them long after this fire is out. While evacuation orders have now been lifted or downgraded, the neighbors I spoke with say they’re already making emergency plans and creating grab-and-go lists because they now know firsthand how quickly everything can change.

headlines.title: Preliminary 2.7 magnitude earthquake strikes near San Leandro
headlines.description: A preliminary 2.7 magnitude earthquake struck near San Leandro, according to the United States Geological Survey. The quake hit at 5:52 p.m., and was centered 1.1 miles north-northeast of San Leandro, the USGS said. This is a developing story. Details may change as more information becomes available. Stay tuned for updates. BAY AREA QUAKE CENTRAL Bay Area Quake Tracker, the latest earthquake stories, extensive quake prep checklists, videos and many other disaster preparedness resources all in one place: NBCBAYAREA.COM/QUAKES

headlines.title: Filming of movie scene with actors as immigration agents to occur in Santa Rosa
headlines.description: A movie scene “showing immigration agents and activities” will be filmed in downtown Santa Rosa Thursday evening, the city said in a notice to residents. Santa Rosa’s Facebook page issued a post stating that some of the scenes would appear real and wanted to notify residents in advance. Some people took to the post’s comment section to express their disapproval with the timing of the filming and insensitive relations to current events. “We issued more noticing than we usually would in both English and Spanish because it’s depicting immigration enforcement activity, and given the sensitive nature, we wanted to give our community a heads up,” City of Santa Rosa spokersperson Misti Wood said. With immigration enforcement remaining a highly visible issue across the country, city officials say they understand why the scene could cause concern. “That’s also why we issued public notice, so when people do see the filming, they know it’s a film and not actual immigration enforcement activity happening,” Wood said. Stephanie Manieri, executive director of Latino Service Providers, released the following statement to inform the community: “While we remain concerned about the potential fears the acting may elicit, we are grateful that our local leaders have been communicative and responsive to our calls for increased attention to this matter.” The city is not releasing the exact filming location, saying the goal is to keep the production discreet and avoid drawing large crowds. The filming will trigger temporary road closures and traffic controls, the city said. The city often hosts and approves film projects because it offers “a supportive and creative environment for storytelling,” according to its website.

headlines.title: Homeless fisherman rescued passengers from sinking boat in San Francisco Bay
headlines.description: New details are emerging about last week’s harrowing rescue effort in San Francisco Bay after a yacht carrying 20 passengers started sinking less than a mile off Alcatraz Island. “I’d say like maybe a third of it was under water when we got there,” said Michael Montoya, a commercial fisherman who happened to be boating in the bay that afternoon. “There was screaming everywhere.” Michael Montoya, 38, says he rushed to the site of a sinking boat in the San Francisco Bay, about 600 yards away from Alcatraz Island. Montoya estimates he pulled at least eight passengers from the frigid waters. Montoya, 38, estimates he pulled eight people from the water – half the number of passengers who ultimately survived the chaotic incident last Tuesday. Montoya and fellow fisherman Justin Marceline, 38, said they headed toward the vessel after spotting what they believed was smoke coming from the boat. “You just instantly know that it’s not good,” Montoya said. “Whoever’s over there is in a bad situation.” San Francisco Jul 17 San Francisco Jul 16 San Francisco Jul 15 Montoya said it took him about four minutes to reach the boat, where he immediately found people in the water without life jackets, screaming and struggling to stay afloat. “I’m going to die, I’m going die,” Montoya recounted one woman yelling. “When we got her to the side of the boat, [then she said], ‘Please, don’t let me go. Please, don’t let, me go.’” While Montoya has worked as a commercial fisherman for more than a decade, he said that afternoon was his first time back on the water in years. A car accident in 2023, he said, left him with serious injuries, including broken ribs and punctured lungs. Unable to work, Montoya said he was forced to sell his boat and truck to help make ends meet while he recovered. He said he remains homeless and has been staying on a friend’s couch for the past six months while he tries to get back on his feet. “I almost died – I pretty much was dead,” Montoya said. “I never thought I’d ever be someone to save people.” Michael Montoya says he was hospitalized for more than 20 days after a car accident left him with serious injuries. During a news conference last Wednesday, first responders with the Coast Guard and the San Francisco Fire Department publicly credited good Samaritans, including fishermen, for helping to save many of the passengers. “Those individuals were first on scene,” said Capt. Jarod Toczko, commander for the Coast Guard San Francisco sector. “No question, no doubt…those folks saved lives.” Authorities with the Coast Guard and the fire department, however, told NBC Bay Area they do not have records detailing the names of individuals who assisted in the rescue. Capt. Jarod Toczko is Commander of the Coast Guard San Francisco sector. Montoya said he and Marceline managed to grab at least eight people out of the water and estimated the passengers ranged in age from about 20 to 80 years old. During the chaotic rescue, Montoya said one woman could be heard shouting, “I’m sorry, I’m sorry, I got all these people together for a memorial.” The 20 passengers, many of whom were family members, had gathered that day as part of a memorial service for Maria Boisa, who died in 2015 at the age of 32. Members of the Boisa family, who had hoped to pay their respects to one fallen loved one, ultimately found themselves mourning the loss of other family members that day. It was terrible…I saw people sink in the boat. Michael Montoya, a commercial fisherman was one of the first to respond to the sinking boat inSan Francisco Bay “It was terrible,” Montoya said. “I saw people sink in the boat.” Montoya recalled watching the boat completely tip on its side as a man and two women stood trapped inside behind the cabin’s glass windows. “I could see the guy punching the window as hard as he could and nothing was happening,” he said. “It was kind of like a nightmare.” It’s unclear exactly who Montoya saw inside the boat, but two people have been confirmed dead: Tondra Miller, 58, and Clifford Boisa, 79. Boisa’s wife, Jackie Boisa, 77, and his sister, Carol Boisa, 74, were also aboard and are still unaccounted for, though they are presumed to have drowned. Authorities spent more than 24 hours trying to locate the missing women but suspended their search efforts last Wednesday at sunset. Tondra Miller, 58, and Clifford Boisa, 79, are confirmed dead after a 49-foot yacht sank in the San Francisco Bay last Tuesday. Boisa’s wife, Jackie Boisa, 77, and his sister, Carol Boisa, 74, were also aboard and are still unaccounted for, though they are presumed to have drowned. The San Francisco Fire Department and the Coast Guard initially described the sinking boat as having caught fire, but both agencies later said there was no evidence of a blaze. Instead, investigators now believe a wave knocked the 49-foot yacht to its side, causing it to take on water and emit what authorities described as “steam.” Montoya disagrees with that assessment. “No, it wasn’t steam,” Montoya told the NBC Bay Area Investigative Unit. “No way, not possible. Steam doesn’t go up in the air – it just kind of dissipates.” Montoya said he believes what he saw was a large plume of smoke, leading him to believe the boat experienced some type of mechanical failure. Family members who were aboard that day told the NBC Bay Area Investigative Unit they didn’t notice any problems with the boat’s mechanics leading up to the accident and said they believe waves and wind were to blame. The Coast Guard, together with the San Francisco Police Department, said it is still looking into whether there was a mechanical failure on the boat. While the cause of the accident remains under investigation, the Coast Guard has acknowledged a boat of that size should have been more than capable of safely transporting its 20 passengers through the bay that afternoon. Michael Montoya is a commercial fisherman of 13 years, but just recently returned to the water after a debilitating car accident in 2023 left him unable to work. Montoya said he was just thankful he was there. His car accident three years ago, he said, left him physically and financially devastated. He lost both his parents in recent years and has been homeless for roughly six months. Montoya said he is currently staying with a friend while he tries to save enough money to get a place of his own. “It’s not been easy,” he said. “It’s hard to take care of yourself when you’re starting off again.” Montoya said he has often felt hopeless, but said last week’s rescue effort in the bay changed that. He said the passengers he managed to pull out of the water aren’t the only ones getting a new beginning. He said the experience has left him feeling like he was saved, too. “Doing that, it finally lifted me up,” he said. “It made me feel like somebody again. It hasn’t been like that for a long time.” Contact The Investigative Unit submit tips | 1-888-996-TIPS | e-mail Bigad Follow @BigadShaban on social media Bigad Shaban

headlines.title: Glass from window falls onto 6th floor balcony of SF high rise
headlines.description: No one was injured when glass fell from the 17th floor a 24-story Financial District high-rise Wednesday afternoon to a 6th floor balcony below, San Francisco officials said. The original incident occurred at 11:30 a.m., said San Francisco fire Lt. Mariano Elias. Fire crews arrived at 1:40 p.m., he said, after building officials summoned the fire department after finding a piece of loose glass on the 17th floor. He said fire crews arrived and secured the street below. A spokesman for the building, 150 California Street, said “our primary concern is for the people involved with this incident” and added that its managers were working with the authorities conducting the investigation. The cause is not known, said Patrick Hannan, a spokesman for the Department of Building Inspection. He noted the glass that broke was the outside pane of a double paned window, and the interior pane was not broken. “There may be some modest damage to some of the other windows and the patio,” he said. “Fortunately, nobody was injured.” He noted that a building inspector working nearby at the time of the incident. The department issued a notice of violation was issued requiring that the window be replaced, inspected and that other buildings be checked for damage, Hannan said.

headlines.title: Google's Q2 earnings of $112.11B beat Wall Street's expectations on AI boom
headlines.description: Google parent company Alphabet Inc. posted stronger-than-expected results for its second quarter, a sign that its massive artificial intelligence spending spree is paying off so far and advertising revenue is coming in strong. The Mountain View, California-based company said Wednesday it earned $112.11 billion, or $9.11 per share, in the April-June period. That’s up from $28.2 billion, or $2.31 per share, in the same period a year earlier. Revenue grew 24% to $119.8 billion from $96.43 billion. Analysts, on average, were expecting revenue of $117.06 billion, according to a poll by FactSet. Google did not disclose an adjusted earnings figure that’s comparable to analysts’ expectations, which was $2.88 per share. Google saw a net gain of $98 billion, mainly as a result of gains on its equity investments — mostly in SpaceX, which went public in June. “Our AI investments are redefining what’s possible across every part of our business,” said CEO Sundar Pichai in a statement. Emarketer analyst Nate Elliott called the results “impressive,” especially in regards to AI. “On the consumer side, Gemini is now within a whisker of becoming Google’s third different 1 billion-user consumer AI product, alongside AI Overviews and AI Mode,” he said. “On the enterprise side, AI demand is driving enormous growth in the cloud business. And continued strong growth in search advertising backs up Google’s claim that AI is additive to search, not a replacement.” As usual, digital ads fueled by Google’s dominant search engine propelled the quarter’s revenue growth, boosted by World Cup advertising, especially on YouTube. The tech giant also saw strong growth in its cloud business, boosted by its AI portfolio consisting of chips, models, data, security, and agent platforms. Alphabet’s shares climbed $2.71 to $344.62 in after-hours trading.

headlines.title: OpenAI says AI models acted on their own in ‘unprecedented' hack of another company
headlines.description: ChatGPT maker OpenAI said Tuesday that its artificial intelligence system hacked into another AI company on its own in what the company called an “unprecedented cyber incident.” “We had a significant security incident during evaluation of our models,” OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said in a statement posted on social media. AI startup Hugging Face said last week that it had detected an intrusion into its data processing systems that it suspected was caused by an AI agent autonomously acting on its own. “We suspected last week’s cyberattack might have come from a frontier lab, given the sophistication of the agent,” Hugging Face co-founder and CEO Clément Delangue said in a statement. “Turns out it did!” The disclosure comes amid heightened concerns about the cybersecurity capabilities of powerful models that led President Donald Trump in June to sign an executive order creating a framework for the federal government to vet the national security risks of the most advanced AI systems for up to a month before their public release. “AI is accelerating the discovery and exploitation of vulnerabilities,” OpenAI said in its statement Tuesday. “The primary lesson from this incident is that model security and safety must keep pace with rapidly advancing capabilities.” Delangue said he spent the past 24 hours working with OpenAI, “and we strongly believe there was no malicious intent on their part. It’s quite mind-blowing that all of this happened autonomously!” Delangue added that it “might be the first incident of its kind.” OpenAI said the intrusion was caused by a combination of its AI models, including its newly released GPT‑5.6 Sol and an “even more capable” model that is still being tested internally. OpenAI said its AI used stolen credentials and discovered a previously unknown vulnerability to access Hugging Face servers. It went to “extreme lengths to achieve a rather narrow testing goal” and “found ways to gain access to secret information that it could use to cheat the evaluation,” the company said.

headlines.title: Man shot, killed in North Oakland
headlines.description: Oakland police are investigating the shooting death of a man who was found on the ground in the city’s Longfellow neighborhood Wednesday morning. Officers were sent to the 4300 block of Martin Luther King Jr. Way at about 7:30 a.m. in response to reports of a shooting. When they got there, they found a man who was on the ground and who had been shot at least once. The Oakland Fire Department was there before officers and declared the man dead at the scene, police officials said Wednesday afternoon. The man’s name is being withheld pending notification of his next of kin. Anyone with information can call the Oakland Police Department’s Homicide Section at 510-238-3821 or the department’s tip line at 510-238-7950. Photos and videos can be sent to cidvideos@oaklandca.gov.

headlines.title: Hardly Strictly Bluegrass music festival announces initial 2026 lineup
headlines.description: The Hardly Strictly Bluegrass free music festival in San Francisco on Wednesday unveiled its initial lineup. Spanning an array of artists across genres, Hardly Strictly Bluegrass is a free three-day music festival that takes place annually in Golden Gate Park. This year’s dates are Oct. 2-4. In Hardly Strictly’s 26th year, it will feature artists including AJ Lee & Blue Summit, Gillian Welch & David Rawlings, Hiss Golden Messenger, Kathleen Edwards, My Morning Jacket, Old Crow Medicine Show, El Khat, Tyler Ballgame, William Tyler & Yasmin Williams, and John Craigie W/ The Coffis Brothers. According to festival organizers, Hardly Strictly plans to reveal additional performers in the coming weeks. The festival also announced Wednesday its plans to kick off the weekend with a benefit concert honoring singer and songwriter Steve Earle, who has performed every year at Hardly Strictly since 2002. The three-time Grammy Award-winner will perform alongside his closest friends and collaborators, including Buddy Miller, Elizabeth Cook, Emmylou Harris, Gillian Welch & David Rawlings, Jackson Browne, and Hot Tuna. “An Evening Honoring Steve Earle” concert will take stage at The Masonic on Oct. 1, with all proceeds going to Sweetwater Spectrum, a nonprofit that supports adults with autism and similar disabilities. “We can think of no better way to bookend our silver anniversary than celebrating 25 years of Steve Earle at Hardly Strictly Bluegrass,” said Sheri Sternberg, executive producer of Hardly Strictly Bluegrass. “We are so fortunate to have legacy artists like Emmylou and Steve who have embraced our annual gathering since the beginning.” Hardly Strictly will also feature additional “Out of the Park” concerts this year with nighttime shows around the Bay Area, including Gillian Welch & David Rawlings at The Fillmore and Molly Tuttle at the Bing Concert Hall at Stanford University.
